draft.introduction: The final question of the Prashna Upanishad concerns the Person with sixteen parts. All parts arise from and resolve into the supreme Self, as rivers merge into the ocean.

translation.text: Sukesha Bharadvaja asked: “Venerable sir, Hiranyanabha, a prince of Kosala, asked this question: Do you know the Person with sixteen parts?” That Person in whom these sixteen parts arise—he is here within the body.
translation.original_text: sukesaḥ ca bhāradvājaḥ papraccha | bhagavan hiraṇyanabhaḥ kausalyaḥ

translation.text: As these flowing rivers, heading toward the ocean, upon reaching the ocean disappear, their name and form destroyed, and are called simply “the ocean”—even so, these sixteen parts of the seer, heading toward the Person, upon reaching the Person disappear.
translation.original_text: yathā imāḥ nadyāḥ syandamnāḥ samudrāyaṇāḥ samudraṃ prāpyastaṃ gacchanti

translator_notes.note: The rivers-into-ocean metaphor became one of the most enduring images in Indian philosophy for the dissolution of individual identity into Brahman. Śaṅkara cites this passage as evidence that liberation involves the complete cessation of individual selfhood.
